summary: The centre-left political party of the United Kingdom, historically the main rival of the Conservative Party after it replaced the Liberal Party as Britain's principal left-of-centre force. Founded via the 1900 Labour Representation Committee with backing from the Fabian Society, it formed its first government under Ramsay MacDonald in 1924, won its first outright majority under Clement Attlee in 1945 (creating the National Health Service), and later governed under Harold Wilson, James Callaghan, Tony Blair, and Gordon Brown before returning to power under Keir Starmer in 2024. Its history includes the 1931 split over the National Government, the 1950s "thirteen wasted years," the 1978-79 Winter of Discontent, the 1983 "longest suicide note in history" manifesto under Michael Foot, Neil Kinnock's 1992 defeat, and Tony Blair's "New Labour"/"Third Way" rebrand.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The party is consistently identified as the centre-left political party of the United Kingdom and the historic rival of the Conservative Party, having replaced the Liberal Party as Britain's main party of the left; when out of government it forms His/Her Majesty's Official Opposition. Giveaway lines most often name-check its long roster of leaders and prime ministers — Ramsay MacDonald, Clement Attlee, Harold Wilson, James Callaghan, Michael Foot, Neil Kinnock, Tony Blair, Gordon Brown, Ed Miliband, Jeremy Corbyn, and Keir Starmer — and its historic Welsh heartland in Merthyr Tydfil and the South Wales valleys.

clues.clue: Identified as the centre-left political party of the United Kingdom, the historic rival/opposition of the Conservative Party, having replaced the Liberal Party as Britain's main party of the left
examples: For 10 points, name this political rival of the Conservative Party.
For 10 points, name this political party that replaced the Liberals as the main centre-left party in Britain.

For 10 points, name this political party, currently led by Ed Miliband, which has produced such figures as Clement Attlee and Tony Blair.

clues.clue: Merthyr Tydfil and the South Wales valleys are historically a heartland for the party
examples: In 2015, Ann Clwyd, an MP from this party, called for Penderyn to be pardoned. Merthyr Tydfil and the South Wales valleys are historically a heartland for this traditionally centre-left UK-wide party.

works.name: Founding: The Fabian Society & the Labour Representation Committee (1900-1906)
works.description: The party emerged from several groups including the Fabian Society, a gradualist socialist organization named for the Roman general "the Delayer" whose members included H.G. Wells and George Bernard Shaw; the Fabians helped found the party through the 1900 Labour Representation Committee. Keir Hardie, elected MP for Merthyr Tydfil in 1900, became the party's first leader after its formal founding six years later.

clues.clue: Emerged from several groups including the Fabian Society (a gradualist socialist organization named for a Punic War general known as "the delayer," whose members included H.G. Wells and George Bernard Shaw), which helped found the party through the namesake 1900 Labour Representation Committee
examples: The Fabian Society helped found this party through its namesake 1900 Representation Committee. This party won its first prime ministry after Ramsay MacDonald defeated Conservative Stanley Baldwin in the 1923 election.
This party emerged from several groups including the Fabian Society, and formed its first government in 1945.

clues.clue: Keir Hardie, elected MP for Merthyr Tydfil in 1900, became the party's first leader after its foundation six years later
examples: Elected as MP for Merthyr Tydfil in 1900, this politician became the first leader of the Labour Party after its foundation six years later.

works.name: The MacDonald Governments & the 1931 Split
works.description: Ramsay MacDonald led the party to its first prime ministry after defeating Conservative Stanley Baldwin in the 1923 election, forming a first, short-lived minority government in 1924 that collapsed after MacDonald halted the prosecution of Communist newspaper editor J.R. Campbell and after the publication of the forged Zinoviev Letter, purportedly written by the president of the Communist International to encourage British communists to press sympathetic Labour forces to back an Anglo-Soviet Treaty. MacDonald returned to power in 1929, but during the financial crisis of 1931 he controversially formed a coalition "National Government" with Stanley Baldwin's Conservatives after facing the Invergordon Mutiny, and was expelled from the party for it; the party lost in a landslide to the National Government, with new leader Arthur Henderson losing 235 seats (including his own, Burnley) and being succeeded by George Lansbury, while Sir Oswald Mosley resigned from the Labour government over the rejection of his namesake Memorandum by the fiscally conservative Chancellor, Philip Snowden, and founded the New Party. Snowden himself used orthodox, Gladstonian tactics against the Great Depression, refusing deficit spending against the urging of John Maynard Keynes.

clues.clue: Ramsay MacDonald led it to its first prime ministry after defeating Conservative Stanley Baldwin in the 1923 election, becoming the party's first prime minister
examples: MacDonald was the first prime minister from this political party, the successor to the Liberal Party which today forms the Official Opposition to the Conservatives.
MacDonald was the first prime minister from this party. Other leaders of this party include James Callaghan, Clement Attlee, and Tony Blair.

clues.clue: MacDonald's first government (1924) fell after he halted the prosecution of Communist newspaper editor J.R. Campbell
examples: This man's party suffered a heavy defeat as a result of his decision to stop the prosecution of a Communist newspaper editor in the Campbell Case.
This prime minister's first term in office lasted just over nine months, since he resigned over the prosecution of the Communist newspaper editor J.R. Campbell.

clues.clue: That first government also fell/was smeared after publication of the forged Zinoviev Letter, purportedly written by the president of the Communist International to press sympathetic Labour forces to back an Anglo-Soviet Treaty
examples: This party, whose members included Aneurin Bevan, lost power after the publication of the Zinoviev Letter while led by Ramsay MacDonald.

This document, now largely discredited as a forgery, was supposedly written by the president of Comintern to encourage communists to press "sympathetic forces" in the Labour Party to back an Anglo-Soviet Treaty.

clues.clue: MacDonald returned to power in 1929, faced the Invergordon Mutiny, and was expelled from the party in 1931 for forming a coalition "National Government" with Stanley Baldwin's Conservatives, leading the party to a landslide defeat

Name this party, defeated in a landslide by the National Government in 1931. The split in this party was driven by its leader, Ramsay MacDonald, forming a coalition with Stanley Baldwin's Conservatives.

works.name: The Attlee Government & the Welfare State (1945-1951)
works.description: The party took power from Winston Churchill's Conservatives in 1945 under Clement Attlee, whose cabinet included Foreign Secretary Ernest Bevin and Minister of Health Aneurin "Nye" Bevan. It created the National Health Service, implementing the 1942 Beveridge Report's blueprint for a welfare state addressing the "five great evils" of want, disease, ignorance, squalor, and idleness, and its 1948 National Assistance Act abolished the last remnants of the workhouse system established under the 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act.

clues.clue: Took power from Winston Churchill's Conservatives in 1945 under Clement Attlee
examples: For 10 points, Harold Wilson and Clement Attlee were members of what British political party, which took power from Winston Churchill's Conservatives in 1945?
This party emerged from several groups including the Fabian Society, and formed its first government in 1945.

clues.clue: Attlee's cabinet included Foreign Secretary Ernest Bevin and Minister of Health Aneurin "Nye" Bevan
examples: Name this 20th-century Prime Minister whose cabinet included Foreign Secretary Ernest Bevin (spelled "B-E-V-I-N") and Minister of Health Aneurin "Nye" Bevan (spelled "B-E-V-A-N").

clues.clue: Created the National Health Service under Attlee's administration
examples: It created the National Health Service during the administration of Clement Attlee.
Attlee belonged to this party, which established Britain's National Health Service under his tenure.

clues.clue: An economist chaired the group that produced the 1942 Beveridge Report, which addressed the "five great evils" of want, disease, ignorance, squalor, and idleness and led to the NHS
examples: An economist from it chaired the group that produced the Beveridge Report, leading to the eventual creation of the National Health Service under this party.
This 1942 document outlined the plan for a British welfare state that would fix the "five great evils" of "want, disease, ignorance, squalor and idleness".

clues.clue: The 1948 National Assistance Act, passed under Attlee, abolished the last remnants of the workhouse system created by the 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act
examples: The last remnants of the workhouse system were abolished by the National Assistance Act of 1948, which was passed during the ministry of Clement Attlee, a leader of this democratic socialist political party.

works.name: The "Thirteen Wasted Years" & Clause Four (1951-1964)
works.description: The party spent the 1950s out of power in what became known as the "thirteen wasted years," split between the left-wing Bevanites, named for Aneurin Bevan (who had resigned as Minister of Labour when funds for social programs were cut to pay for rearmament), and the moderate Gaitskellites. After losing the 1959 election — in which the Conservatives urged voters not to let Labour "ruin" their lives — leader Hugh Gaitskell tried to alter Clause Four, the party's constitutional commitment to public ownership of the means of production.

clues.clue: Spent the 1950s (the "thirteen wasted years") out of power, split between the Bevanites and the moderate Gaitskellites
examples: The "thirteen wasted years" in which this party was out of power occurred in the 1950s and saw a split between Bevanites and moderate Gaitskellites.

clues.clue: Aneurin Bevan, Minister of Labour under Attlee, resigned when funds for social programs were cut to pay for rearmament, lending his name to the party's more radical wing in the 1950s
examples: This Minister of Labour for Clement Atlee resigned when funds for social programs were cut to pay for rearmament. He lends his name to the more radical wing of the Labour Party in the 1950s.

clues.clue: In the 1959 election, the Conservatives urged voters not to let the party "ruin" their lives, and Labour lost
examples: A 1959 election saw an opposing party ask voters not to let this party "ruin" their lives.

clues.clue: After the 1959 defeat, leader Hugh Gaitskell led a movement to alter Clause Four, the manifesto's overtly socialist statement that workers should control the means of production
examples: This controversial portion of the party's manifesto contained an overtly socialist message in its statement that workers should control the means of production. Hugh Gaitskell led a movement to alter this statement after losing the 1959 general election.

works.name: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)
works.description: Harold Wilson served non-consecutive terms as prime minister in the 1960s and 70s, keeping Britain out of the Vietnam War and opposing Enoch Powell's "Rivers of Blood" speech, though his government's Beeching cuts drastically reduced the British railway network over his own party's opposition; a Wilson-era prime minister was also targeted by the alleged "Clockwork Orange" conspiracy (per the book Spycatcher) and drew criticism for handing out peerages on the "Lavender List." The party legalized abortion under Home Secretary Roy Jenkins, but James Callaghan's government collapsed after the 1978-79 "Winter of Discontent" strikes — mocked by The Sun's "Crisis? What Crisis?" headline — leading to defeat by Margaret Thatcher's Conservatives in 1979.

clues.clue: Harold Wilson served non-consecutive terms as PM in the 1960s and 70s, kept Britain out of the Vietnam War, and opposed Enoch Powell's "Rivers of Blood" speech
examples: This Labour PM served nonconsecutive terms in the 1960s and 70s. He avoided the Vietnam War and opposed Enoch Powell's "Rivers of Blood speech."

clues.clue: Wilson's government oversaw the Beeching cuts, massive reductions to the British railway network, despite opposition from his own party
examples: A later Labour prime minister, Harold Wilson, oversaw these massive reductions of the British railroad network, despite opposition from his party. They followed a Conservative namesake's "report" on The Reshaping of British Railways.

clues.clue: A Labour prime minister was allegedly targeted by the "Clockwork Orange" conspiracy, thought (per the book Spycatcher) to be aimed at a foreign agent; the same era saw a Labour PM's controversial "Lavender List" of resignation honours, including peerages for businessmen thought to be corrupt
examples: A Prime Minister from this party raised eyebrows by giving peerages to those on the "Lavender List," including several businessmen thought to be corrupt.
The alleged "Clockwork Orange" campaign was supposedly directed against a Prime Minister from this party, who, according to the book Spycatcher, was thought to be a foreign agent.

clues.clue: Legalized abortion under Home Secretary Roy Jenkins, per Tony Judt's Postwar
examples: Tony Judt's Postwar notes that the "decline of the moral authority of the British establishment" began with the trial, and continued with this party's legalization of abortion under Home Secretary Roy Jenkins. This party lost the 1979 general election to the Conservatives.

clues.clue: James Callaghan's government collapsed after the 1978-79 "Winter of Discontent" strikes; The Sun mocked his indifference with the headline "Crisis? What Crisis?"
examples: A member of this party was criticized by the headline "Crisis? What Crisis?" during the (*) Winter of Discontent.
The Sun ran the headline "Crisis? What Crisis?" to parody the indifference of Jim Callaghan, this party's leader, during the "Winter of Discontent."

clues.clue: Lost the 1979 general election to Margaret Thatcher's Conservatives, driven out of power after the Winter of Discontent
examples: This party thus lost the ensuing election to Margaret Thatcher's party.
After being driven out thanks to the 1978 "Winter of Discontent," this party was the minority from 1980 to 1997, continually losing to the party of John Major.

works.name: Opposition Under Foot and Kinnock (1979-1992)
works.description: Out of power throughout the party's so-called "Wilderness Years" (1979-1997), the party was led first by Michael Foot, under whom it fought the 1983 election on a manifesto — dubbed "the longest suicide note in history" by Gerald Kaufman — that called for withdrawal from the European Economic Community and abolition of the House of Lords; the leftward shift also drove a moderate "Gang of Four" led by David Owen to break away and found the Social Democratic Party. Neil Kinnock then led the party, defeating an attempted infiltration by the hard-left Militant Tendency and abandoning the largely symbolic Clause Four, but lost the 1992 election to John Major after the "War of Jennifer's Ear" advertising scandal and hostile tabloid coverage from The Sun, which mocked him with the "will the last person to leave Britain please turn out the lights" headline and later boasted "It's The Sun Wot Won It."

clues.clue: Out of power throughout its so-called "Wilderness Years" (1979-1997), during the 1980s led by Michael Foot and then Neil Kinnock
examples: During the 1980s, this party was led by Michael Foot and Neil Kinnock.
This party's "Wilderness Years" lasted from 1979 until 1997, and it issued the manifesto "Let Us Face the Future" near the end of World War II.

clues.clue: Its 1983 election manifesto, drafted principally by Michael Foot and dubbed "the longest suicide note in history" by Gerald Kaufman, called for withdrawal from the European Economic Community and abolition of the House of Lords
examples: They issued "the longest suicide note in history," a manifesto for an election in 1983, under (*) Michael Foot's leadership.
Kaufman was talking about what political document principally drafted by men like Michael Foot? This 1983 document called for proposals such as a withdrawal from the European Economic Community.

clues.clue: The party's leftward shift in the 1980s drove a moderate "Gang of Four," led by David Owen, to break away and found the Social Democratic Party, which later merged with the Liberal Party
examples: The shift left by Labour during the 1980s caused a moderate "Gang of Four" led by David Owen to break away and found a party with this name, which later merged with the Liberal Party.

clues.clue: Under Neil Kinnock, abandoned its largely symbolic Clause Four and defeated an attempted infiltration by the hard-left Militant Tendency
examples: This party abandoned its largely symbolic Clause Four and defeated an attempted infiltration by the Militant Tendency.

clues.clue: Lost the 1992 election to John Major's Conservatives after the "War of Jennifer's Ear" advertising scandal accused the party of exploiting children
examples: It was accused of exploiting children in the "War of Jennifer's Ear" scandal.
It lost the 1992 election because of misleading adverts during the War of Jennifer's Ear.

clues.clue: The Sun tabloid mocked leader Neil Kinnock before the 1992 election with the headline asking "will the last person to leave Britain please turn out the lights," then boasted "It's The Sun Wot Won It" after Labour's defeat; Kinnock was separately mocked for shouting "We're all right!" at a pre-election rally
examples: The Sun boasted "It's The Sun Wot Won It" after its headline "If [this politician] wins today, will the last person to leave Britain please turn out the lights?"
A leader of this party was mocked for shouting "We're all right!" multiple times during a 1992 rally.

works.name: New Labour: Blair and Brown (1994-2010)
works.description: Tony Blair took the party in a "New" direction in the 1990s, adopting a "Third Way" strategy to appeal to centrists and formally rewriting Clause Four; the party returned to government under Blair and then Gordon Brown, whose ranks during this era included brothers David and Ed Miliband.

clues.clue: Tony Blair took the party in a "New" direction in the 1990s, adopting a "Third Way" strategy to appeal to centrists
examples: In the early 1990s, it adopted a "Third Way" strategy to appeal to centrists.
It was taken in a "New" direction by Tony Blair in the 1990s.

clues.clue: Governed under Tony Blair and then Gordon Brown; David and Ed Miliband were both members during this era
examples: Both David and Edward are members of this slightly leftist British party that was in power under the previous two Prime Ministers, Gordon Brown and Tony Blair.

works.name: Opposition Under Miliband and Corbyn (2010-2020)
works.description: Ed Miliband became leader in 2010, defeating his brother David (who went on to serve as Shadow Foreign Secretary) in the party's leadership election. Jeremy Corbyn later led the party — described in one clue as a "radical pacifist vegetarian" — joining a broader rise of European left-wing movements alongside Greece's SYRIZA and Spain's Podemos, before being removed as leader amid accusations of fostering antisemitism within the party and continuing as an independent MP for Islington North.

clues.clue: Ed Miliband became leader in 2010, defeating his brother David, who became Shadow Foreign Secretary while Ed led the Shadow Cabinet as Leader of the Opposition (having formerly been Gordon Brown's Secretary of Energy and Climate Change)
examples: This man is the current leader of the Labour Party, having defeated his brother David in an election for the position in 2010.

This member of Her Majesty's Loyal Opposition currently leads the British shadow cabinet. He was formerly Gordon Brown's Secretary of Energy and Climate Change. His brother, David, serves as Shadow Foreign Minister.

clues.clue: Jeremy Corbyn, described as a "radical pacifist vegetarian," led the party as part of a broader rise of European left-wing movements alongside Greece's SYRIZA and Spain's Podemos
examples: Joining SYRIZA and Podemos in the recent rise of left-wing movements in Europe is Jeremy Corbyn, the radical pacifist vegetarian leader of this mainstream party in Britain, which opposes the Conservatives.

clues.clue: Corbyn was removed as leader over accusations of fostering antisemitism and continued as an independent MP representing Islington North
examples: An independent representing Islington North was removed as leader of this party over accusations of fostering antisemitism.

works.name: The Starmer Era (2020-present)
works.description: Keir Starmer took over the party's leadership from Jeremy Corbyn and led it to a landslide 2024 general election victory that removed Rishi Sunak's Conservative government, though the party's subsequent 2025 local elections produced its worst-ever result, finishing fourth. Starmer's tenure has also seen internal turmoil, from Rosie Duffield's resignation over hospitality-donation allegations and a gender-related dispute, to a jailed MP's forced resignation, an unpopular dancing deputy leader, the loss of seats to independents over the party's stance on Gaza, and a refusal to back a mayoral candidate who appeared alongside a filmmaker expelled from the party.

clues.clue: Keir Starmer took over the party's leadership from Jeremy Corbyn

clues.clue: Under Starmer, won a landslide 2024 general election victory that removed Rishi Sunak's Conservative government
examples: In 2025 local elections, this party had its worst-ever result, finishing fourth, despite winning a landslide victory nationally less than a year prior to remove Rishi Sunak's government.

clues.clue: Despite the 2024 landslide, suffered its worst-ever result in the 2025 local elections, finishing fourth
examples: In 2025 local elections, this party had its worst-ever result, finishing fourth.

clues.clue: Rosie Duffield quit the party after Starmer was alleged to have abused corporate hospitality donations, having also clashed with him over the "For Women" case
examples: Rosie Duffield, who quit this party after its leader was alleged to have abused corporate hospitality donations, has clashed with its leader over the "For Women" case.

clues.clue: MP Mike Amesbury was forced to quit in March 2025 after being jailed for assaulting a constituent
examples: This party's member Mike Amesbury was forced to quit in March 2025 after being jailed for beating up a constituent.

clues.clue: In 2024, the party's deputy leader was criticized after being filmed dancing in a nightclub to Gotye's "Somebody That I Used to Know"
examples: In 2024, this party's deputy leader was criticised after being filmed dancing in a nightclub to Gotye's "Somebody That I Used To Know."

clues.clue: Four independent candidates backed by "The Muslim Vote" defeated party members, including in the former seat of George Galloway, after criticizing its stance on Palestine
examples: Four independent candidates backed by (*) Muslim Vote defeated members of this political party, the former home of George Galloway, after criticizing its stance on Palestine.

clues.clue: Refused to support Jamie Driscoll in a mayoral election after he appeared onstage with filmmaker Ken Loach, who had been expelled from the party for joining "Labour Against the Witchhunt"
examples: This party refused to support Jamie Driscoll in a mayoral election after he appeared onstage with Ken Loach, who was expelled for joining an organization named "[this party] Against the Witchhunt."

clues.clue: In Tower Hamlets, after Lutfur Rahman was removed as mayor for electoral fraud, he was replaced by the party's John Biggs
examples: After becoming Britain's first mayor to be removed due to electoral fraud, Lutfur Rahman was replaced by John Biggs, a member of this party.

comprehensive_summary: The Labour Party is the centre-left political party of the United Kingdom, historically the main rival of the Conservative Party after it replaced the Liberal Party as Britain's principal party of the left; when out of government it forms His/Her Majesty's Official Opposition. It emerged from several groups including the Fabian Society — a gradualist socialist organization named for the Roman general "the Delayer" whose members included H.G. Wells and George Bernard Shaw — which helped found the party through the 1900 Labour Representation Committee; Keir Hardie, elected MP for Merthyr Tydfil in 1900, became the party's first leader after its formal founding six years later, and Merthyr Tydfil and the South Wales valleys remain a historic party heartland. Ramsay MacDonald led the party to its first prime ministry after defeating Conservative Stanley Baldwin in the 1923 election, but his first, minority government (1924) collapsed after he halted the prosecution of Communist newspaper editor J.R. Campbell and following the publication of the forged Zinoviev Letter, purportedly written by the president of the Communist International to press sympathetic Labour forces to back an Anglo-Soviet Treaty. MacDonald returned to power in 1929, but amid the financial crisis of 1931 — after facing the Invergordon Mutiny — he controversially formed a coalition "National Government" with Stanley Baldwin's Conservatives and was expelled from the party; Labour lost in a landslide, with new leader Arthur Henderson losing 235 seats (including his own, Burnley) before being succeeded by George Lansbury, while Sir Oswald Mosley resigned from the Labour government after the rejection of his namesake Memorandum by fiscally conservative Chancellor Philip Snowden (who himself used orthodox, Gladstonian tactics against the Great Depression, resisting deficit spending against John Maynard Keynes's advice) and founded the New Party. The party took power from Winston Churchill's Conservatives in 1945 under Clement Attlee, whose cabinet included Foreign Secretary Ernest Bevin and Minister of Health Aneurin "Nye" Bevan; it created the National Health Service, implementing the 1942 Beveridge Report's blueprint for a welfare state addressing the "five great evils" of want, disease, ignorance, squalor, and idleness, and its 1948 National Assistance Act abolished the last remnants of the workhouse system created by the 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act. The party then spent the 1950s out of power in the "thirteen wasted years," split between the left-wing Bevanites (named for Bevan, who had resigned as Minister of Labour over rearmament-driven cuts to social programs) and the moderate Gaitskellites; after losing the 1959 election, leader Hugh Gaitskell tried to alter Clause Four, the party's constitutional commitment to public ownership of the means of production. Harold Wilson served non-consecutive terms as prime minister in the 1960s and 70s, keeping Britain out of the Vietnam War and opposing Enoch Powell's "Rivers of Blood" speech, though his government's Beeching cuts drastically reduced the British railway network over his own party's objections, and a Wilson-era Labour prime minister was allegedly targeted by the "Clockwork Orange" conspiracy while also drawing criticism for the "Lavender List" of resignation honours. The party legalized abortion under Home Secretary Roy Jenkins, but James Callaghan's government collapsed after the 1978-79 "Winter of Discontent" strikes — mocked by The Sun's "Crisis? What Crisis?" headline — leading to defeat by Margaret Thatcher's Conservatives in 1979 and beginning the party's so-called "Wilderness Years" (1979-1997). Under Michael Foot, the party fought the 1983 election on a manifesto dubbed "the longest suicide note in history" by Gerald Kaufman, calling for withdrawal from the European Economic Community and abolition of the House of Lords, while its leftward shift drove a moderate "Gang of Four" led by David Owen to break away and found the Social Democratic Party. Neil Kinnock then led the party, defeating an attempted infiltration by the hard-left Militant Tendency and abandoning the largely symbolic Clause Four, but lost the 1992 election to John Major after the "War of Jennifer's Ear" advertising scandal and hostile Sun tabloid coverage that mocked him with the "will the last person to leave Britain please turn out the lights" headline before boasting "It's The Sun Wot Won It." Tony Blair then took the party in a "New" direction in the 1990s, adopting a "Third Way" strategy to appeal to centrists and formally rewriting Clause Four, returning Labour to power under Blair and then Gordon Brown, whose ranks included brothers David and Ed Miliband. Ed Miliband became leader in 2010, defeating David (who became Shadow Foreign Secretary); Jeremy Corbyn later led the party — described in one clue as a "radical pacifist vegetarian" — as part of a broader rise of European left-wing movements alongside Greece's SYRIZA and Spain's Podemos, before being removed as leader amid accusations of fostering antisemitism and continuing as an independent MP for Islington North. Keir Starmer took over the leadership from Corbyn and led the party to a landslide 2024 general election victory that removed Rishi Sunak's Conservative government, though the 2025 local elections produced Labour's worst-ever result, finishing fourth; Starmer's tenure has also seen Rosie Duffield's resignation over hospitality-donation allegations and a gender-related dispute, MP Mike Amesbury's forced resignation after a jailing, criticism of a dancing deputy leader, the loss of seats to independents backed by "The Muslim Vote" over the party's stance on Gaza, a refusal to back mayoral candidate Jamie Driscoll after he appeared alongside filmmaker Ken Loach (expelled from the party), and the replacement of fraud-ousted Tower Hamlets mayor Lutfur Rahman by Labour's John Biggs.
recursive_suggestions: Excluded: the 2015 SHEIKH tossup on 'Australian Labour Party' (Night of the Long Prawns, Jack Lang breakaways, Snowy Mountains Scheme, Gough Whitlam's dismissal, Ben Chifley/Julia Gillard) and the 2020 ACF Regionals bonus part on 'Australian Labor Party' (Bob Hawke, Joh for Canberra) were excluded — different answerline/topic from the UK Labour Party despite the shared name
Excluded: the 2024 Booster Shot bonus on 'New Zealand Labour Party' (Rogernomics, Roger Douglas, David Lange, Ruth Richardson/'Ruthanasia') was excluded entirely — different answerline/topic

Excluded: the 2019 Chicago Open bonus part on the (Northern Irish) 'Social Democratic and Labour Party' (John Hume, Gerry Adams, SDLP) was excluded — a distinct party despite sharing 'Labour' in its name
Fabian Society — recurring answerline (named its own bonus part; H.G. Wells, George Bernard Shaw, Annie Besant, Beatrice and Sidney Webb) that could merit its own topic page
Ramsay MacDonald — recurring individual figure answerline (2x own bonus-part answer per the digest; Zinoviev Letter, Invergordon Mutiny, National Government); could merit a standalone figure page
Clement Attlee — recurring individual figure answerline (own bonus-part answer); 1945 government, NHS, Ernest Bevin/Aneurin Bevan cabinet
Winter of Discontent — recurring event referenced across several questions (Callaghan, 'Crisis? What Crisis?', 1979 defeat); could merit its own topic if it accumulates more independent presence
Aneurin 'Nye' Bevan — recurring figure (Minister of Health under Attlee, namesake of the Bevanites) currently folded into this page
